speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEPING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 18, 1995. We're here in Branford, Connecticut in the United States of America to interview Chaim Engel, who's a survivor of Sobibor and part of the resistance. This interview will be conducted in English. What is your full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim Engel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: January the 10th, 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel and Frieda Engel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a brother nine years younger than me, and his name was Meier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meier. Do you remember your life growing up in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, partly, I guess. After so many years, it's not so fresh in my memory. But I think I remember certain things.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was not born in Lodz. I was born in a small place [? next ?] [? by ?] [? Posnan, ?] and when I was 5 years, my parents moved to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's job?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a business. Well, the place where he lived, the reason he moved is he had that textile store in a small place. And he probably made just a living, but they called him the millionaire. Well, it was very far from it, but it was the jealousy of the not-Jewish people that somebody had a store. And because of that, my father moved to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you orthodox in your upbringing? Did you keep a Jewish home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had a Jewish home. My father was really a [? finished ?] [? rabbi, ?] although he didn't practice this [? habit. ?] He was really a Jewish scholar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember holidays, what holidays were like in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, vaguely. Your basic holidays. Holidays was always a time for festivity, and mostly what you remember is the Passover, the bigger things. And holidays you had to go to the temple always, like Yom Kippur and all the other holidays. We just felt like a Jewish family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to a regular elementary school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah. I was-- there was a school what was a really-- you have the requirements of the government you have to have the elementary education, but it was connected with a Jewish school that you learn also a Jewish learning. So that is the kind of school I participated. I was in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spoke Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. You learned about, but not speaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Did you feel any anti-Semitism when you were a teenager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to be honest, I didn't have too much to do with it when I lived in Lodz, which is a bigger city, I was-- I didn't-- I didn't have friends with really-- much friends, many friends, would have been not Jews. I remember in Lodz there was an [INAUDIBLE] in the city, but I wouldn't go as a Jew there because I knew it is a not-Jewish neighborhood. And I was really afraid to go there, whether it was a reason, but just that we felt that way. So it was a lot of anti-Semitism, but I personally didn't experience it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did things change when Hitler invaded Poland? How did that change your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was, in the time when Hitler came, I was a soldier in the Polish army. I was already one and a half year in the army, so I don't know what had truly changed, but the whole Poland is gone-- was gone. They occupied in three weeks, and I was-- three weeks I was in the war, in the war as a Polish soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you went into the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, probably 19. 19, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you drafted, or did you volunteer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, drafted. No, drafted. Everybody had to be one and a half year in the army, and that was my time I went there. And I am supposed to be freed on November the 15th, was my time expired, but the war broke out September the 1st, so it caught me in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now once the war ended, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three weeks later, I was-- became a prisoner of war. And they took me to Germany with all prisoners of war. And I was in Germany till March, half March, when they decided to send all Jewish prisoners of war back to Poland because they had already the plans for the Jews, so they didn't keep them as prisoners of war in Poland-- in Germany. They sent them back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this when you were sent back from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940. When I came back, my parents didn't-- were not more in their house, and I found out that they sent them away, with more Jews, sent away to-- on the side of Lublin-- a town, a small place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they put in a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in a ghetto. They put them in small places where all the Jews were there. And I somehow found out where they were, and I went there. And oh, they had a very hard time because they had to leave everything. They had with them only what they could carry, so they came with nothing. And they had a very, very hard time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came there, I went to work at the farm there because there was one of the few places which legally you could work as a Jew [? at ?] somebody. So I work at the [INAUDIBLE] farmer as a help. My brother did the same thing. He went to working too. And uh, with working there, I had shelter and-- and food, and I also could help my parents a little bit with the-- When did things change there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the minute I came back it changed, because everything was different. There were more restrictions. Jews were not allowed to go any places. They weren't allowed to work with everyone. There was not business anymore. There was-- started to be restrictions on everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear a Star of David?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, surely. We wore it on the armband, a Star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you taken out of Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I say, I worked-- I worked as the farmer, and we worked as long as Jews were allowed still to work. And one day was a decree for the Germans that all Jews have to concentrate in the nearest town. So I didn't want to go to the town, but I knew already what will happen. And with my brother and a friend that also worked at the farm, a Jewish friend, we decided to go in the woods, live in the woods instead of the-- instead of go to the city. But we didn't have any cooperation from the non-Jewish population. We didn't have any food, so we had to just come out. So we went to a place, Izbica, which is another city in Poland. And there were still some Jews. And we arrived there. The same evening, they took all the Jews out of their houses and took them for transport. Now we were hiding in the attic in the hope that the Germans would go, and then come out, but they did not leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all of the Jews which were hidden, what didn't come out in the first place, they were taken to transport. And that is the way, when we came out, they took us all. And that is the way I came to Sobibor. What was your first impressions of Sobibor when you arrived, Chaim? Did you know about the camps and the gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. Well, you see there was rumor-- there were rumors that they gassed the people, they kill the people, but a decent human being cannot imagine that something like that really is true. And you always had the feeling where just imagining things because people just talking [? about it. ?] We just couldn't believe it, so we didn't believe the idea that it's true what they say.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came in a camp, and it was-- the impression, I don't know how to say it. We knew we come to a camp. We didn't know what to expect. And we stand in the [? long ?] line, and they pick out 20. They even didn't ask you for profession, like you saw in the movie. He said, where do you come from? I say from Lodz. They said out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I happened to be with another-- about 20 people that were picked out. I didn't know [? if this is ?] working [? or what would happen. ?] And I was picked out and it happened to be for work. But my brother and my friends what was in the same transport, they went to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you were selected to work, were you taken to the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was taken to the barracks. And then we started to talk with the inmates what were already there, and then we knew what's going on here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first work you were made to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same afternoon, they took me to separate the clothes, what we did. And while I was separating the clothes, I found the clothes of my brother and pictures from him, so I knew what's going-- what happened. Sure, it was like somebody hit me on my head, what happened. Because as I say, we never could believe that somebody, like I said, is really possible. And still, all the years [? through, ?] all the time we were in camp, we knew what's going on. We saw it with our own eyes. [? Well, ?] still you feel this-- we're dreaming, it's not true, because you couldn't get the idea that people do things like that to people. But sadly enough, it was true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you worked in sorting?

speaker: interviewee
text: My basic work was sorting clothes, but if the transport came-- and they picked you sometimes to do all kind of works. Happened sometimes after transport came, they picked people to cut the hair from the women. What it was is next to the barracks were the gas chambers, where there were barracks for the women. And they randomly cut the long hair, what they used to sell it to-- later, to send back to Germany for brushes and all kind of stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they pick you. We were maybe 5 feet away from the gas chamber, the barrack was so near to. And you couldn't say anything to the people. You knew what's going on. You couldn't help them. And if you talk with them, they take you too and you go with them. So really you couldn't say anything. You just-- you just keep quiet and did what they told you to do. The book about Sobibor says that you developed a system to survive that you eventually taught to Selma.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, really I believe, really, if I could be not in the front, be-- not be obvious, not seeing you. Stay out of everything. Just if he goes, the German, goes here, you go there. So [? there's ?] not much [? touch ?] with them. That was really, I think, what I-- when I said that, that's what I had in mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the first time that the Dutch Jews arrived and you saw Selma? Do you remember that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember exactly the-- that Selma arrived with the transport. Not really. I don't know how to say it. I have many special impressions for the first transport what arrived from the Dutch Jews. It was always a sad experience when you saw a new transport where people came, and we know-- we knew where they're going. So that was always a sad experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the groups of Jews mix? You were a Polish Jew, Selma was Dutch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you mixed and you didn't mix. I had some Polish friends. Because I was with Selma, I had some people, the Dutch people, what I know. But mostly, people tried to-- it was easy, also, with the language. You could easily communicate with them. And although some different experiences-- see, the Polish Jews experienced differently than the Dutch Jews. The Polish Jews knew more what's going on, all the same situation as me. Nobody believed it, but later you knew where you are. Like when the Polish women went to hair-- cut hair, cut the hair, they were yelling. They felt already what's going on, and they felt always very sad-- not sadly, but yelling and screaming and things like that. The Dutch women, they still believed it's just what they told them. They go to a camp working, and they cut the hair because the infection of-- they get lice or whatever. So there was a different reaction by the not-- the Dutch Jews than the Polish Jews or the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were working sorting, did you often come across food or gold or coins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, surely. The basic thing was not [? longer ?] sorting the clothes. We had to check all the clothes, all of this money in or food, because the people what were brought on transport brought with them-- they could take only what they could carry. Because of that, everybody didn't know where he goes or she goes, so they took all the valuables and make it as small as possible. So they sew it in the clothes, like gold, all those things like that. And then all of these things went through our hands. But we sabotaged. We brought a whole lot to the fire where they'd burn the waste. So with all the papers we do to just to sabotage that. But you couldn't do too much because it was obvious, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So food [? was ?] [? it. ?] We were not allowed to use the food, take the food. If you took it and you did it, they caught you, you got shot. But regardless, we did it because we had to survive. Because the food in the camp was so bad that nobody could really survive off it, and a lot of people died in a very short time.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, in the morning, black water what's supposed to be coffee. And then in afternoon, we got a slice of bread about an inch wide and a so-called soup. Now we called it 'spit soup.' It was a soup [INAUDIBLE], the corn with the skin, with everything so you had to spit it out. Some people didn't eat it, and they really died [INAUDIBLE]. But that was the only food what you got to eat, so you had to steal all this food.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the barracks at night with the other young men, did you ever talk about home? I mean, what sustained you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't think we talked about home. We talk about the situation where we are in, and talk a lot-- they kept you so busy, you always had so much happening every minute of every day of it, so you really very seldom talk about the life in normal before you came to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first hear anyone speaking about the uprising and the plan to resist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a long time was talking about uprising. But well, first of all, was a very secret thing, because people talk sometimes, and it comes quick out. And if it comes out, even a suspicion, it would killed us all. So there was no talk. And I was really not originally involved in it because I worked with Selma, who was Dutch. And even not all the Polish ones were involved in it because, as I say, it was a big secret. But I had friends which were inside, and I was always up what's going on. So I didn't have any assignments, because the assignments of the uprising was that people on the work will kill some of the Germans. Well, I was not assigned to that because, as I say, officially I didn't know what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe-- what was the date of the uprising, Chaim?

speaker: interviewee
text: October the 14th, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what happened that morning at 4 o'clock, what you told Selma to do and what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew that is the day when it happened, so we went normally to work like always. [? And ?] the only thing is through the time, we put away valuables. The place where they burned the refuse-- papers, things like that-- I had the friend-- he was a friend of mine what [? was ?] [? a ?] working.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I always gave him valuables, and I put them-- took them and put them in the ground. When it is a day, we go to run away, I go to take it with me. So the same day, already took-- The day before, I took all these things that was valuables, and I bandaged it on my leg because I couldn't keep it on me. So I bandaged [? with ?] bandages on my leg, and that is the way I took with me valuables. And also, we took from the clothes what we were sorting. We took some better clothes, because we wore-- we wore regular clothes, we didn't-- You did not wear uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any camp stuff. We had regular clothes, so we could, if a jacket was a good jacket, you took a jacket or whatever. And we took maybe two jackets with two pants or whatever because we knew today is the day we run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we worked. And while we're working, there was already going on-- there was the places where the tailor worked, the goldsmith, all these people. They worked for the Germans. They made for them clothes, shoes, things like that. So they were so organized and [? of ?] [? these ?] days, they supposed to come for a fitting or whatever. And there were already people there assigned, when they come, to kill them. And so there was going on, the whole day was it going on, and there were killed a lot of people. And we knew all the time, because there were people which were-- their work was to move from place to place, and they always told us how far the uprising progressed. And we knew there, already so many got killed. So there was tension, surely. There was tension-- afraid that it doesn't come out. You kept quiet. You didn't talk about, and you did always like nothing happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then where I worked, it was the warehouses from clothes, so the Germans would supervise us, or they chaperoned us, or whatever you call it. They were called with a pretense to the magazine, and they got killed there. But still the work went through like nothing happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you called to play a part that you didn't plan to play because someone else couldn't do the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. There-- there was a-- there was in an office was a German what was the plan that he has to be killed, because it was a very important position. He was in a place where everything had to go through there. So if he would have caught something happened, that would be the end of it. There were two people assigned to go there. At once, one of them got afraid and didn't want to go. And so I figured we have 10-- in that time, around 10 were already killed of the Germans, and if we stopped now, [? then ?] for sure dead. So I'm not a big hero, I guess. But I think my brains dictated me, just go. Do it. Otherwise, we were lost. And I went with the other fellow. I went in the office. And Selma gave me a knife, like a regular kitchen knife. The movie shows a fancy knife, but it was not a fancy knife. It was a regular pointy kitchen knife. It was not far from the magazine where they collected all the utensils, all the kind of stuff. So we were standing there. And she came with a knife. And I went with the other fellow. It was a younger fellow, [NON-ENGLISH]. He was the [? four-- ?] three brothers and a father, it was again. He was a very energetic fellow. He killed a few even before that. So we went in there, and we killed a German.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a knife and I jabbed him with the knife. I say here's for my father, for my mother, for my brother, and for all the Jews you killed there. That's the part that I took up. And then I was full of blood. I cut my wrist, [? just ?] here. Cut my wrist, and I was full of blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came out of the office, and Selma was there because she knew where I am. And she, with her handkerchief, she wiped up the blood off my face and then put on a handkerchief on my wrist. And it was the time to go back to the camp. So although so many killed and going on, we still did like nothing happened because we marched back to the living quarters the same way as always, just marching back. That was my part that I had in the uprising.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now once you were back to camp, when did you know its was safe to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I came, there was already more people arrived from other places [? that ?] were. And that was a big confusion, a big-- what is the right word for it? A big confusion. And people start to ran all directions. Some ran on the mines and got killed there. Some ran to the front gate, got killed there. And we were standing. We start to all muscle people. People were confused, didn't know what to do. And I grabbed Selma's hand. And there was a German, [? Frenzel, ?] was in the front with a machine gun. And he put a bucket on his head, because there were some of the uprisers had some guns and they were shooting, too. So I grabbed Selma's hand, and I said-- I figured we have to get out. I mean, there's no sense waiting. So I grabbed her hand and I ran to the front gate, and somehow we were lucky. We made it through the front gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now once you went through the gate, where did you go? Into the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was still about a quarter mile from to camp gate till the woods. And it was drizzling, raining. So we ran as fast as we could to the woods, and we came in the woods. And Selma had a boot and somehow was wrong [? with it. ?] She had to take it off. And it was so wet, she [? kept ?] putting on the boot. So she had the boot and half of the boot out, so she ran with a half boot on. And while we are running, there was-- we found-- later, walking in the woods, we found a group of other inmates, a whole group of them. And so we thought we stay together with them. But the one what had a rifle, he didn't agree on that. He didn't want that we stay there. And I assume the reason is that Selma didn't speak Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because she was Dutch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and Selma left by yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We left by ourself. And I was thinking, because I worked before the war as a farmer, so I was thinking well, we'll try. Maybe we can get in this direction. And there was nice people that I worked for them, they might be able to help me. I was not too sure they would have helped us, but that's the only thing I could think of at the time. So that didn't work even this way because in mean time it got early in the morning, and we had to hide because then it's day, and we knew they were looking all over. So we--
